posture: Commencement eve in NSW: with the reform package taking effect from 4pm 30 June / 1 July, the live signals are a pre-commencement rush of claims lodged under the old rules, mounting evidence that tighter eligibility is pushing mental-injury claims into superannuation, and a step-change in psychosocial enforcement exposure.
summary: The headline reform package (revised SIRA Guidelines, frozen premium target, new employer excess, tighter psychological-injury test) was captured in the 28 June digest and is not repeated. Today's new signals are forward-looking: lawyers and clinicians report a surge of claims being notified before 1 July to preserve access under the existing scheme, which icare and the Personal Injury Commission must absorb; an EML-commissioned University of Melbourne white paper warns the tightening across schemes is redirecting mental-injury claims into superannuation income-protection and TPD; and NSW psychosocial enforcement hardens as codes of practice become an enforceable duty and unions gain standing to bring civil-penalty proceedings. No verifiable fresh AFR or SMH public headline surfaced in the past 24 hours, so no paywalled items are captured today.

items.headline: Pre-commencement lodgement rush as workers race to claim under the old rules before 1 July
items.summary: Legal and clinical commentary ahead of the 1 July changeover points to a wave of psychological-injury claims being notified before commencement, because claims lodged before 1 July are generally assessed under the existing, more generous thresholds. Commentators note only around 88 workers were assessed at 25% WPI or above in the 2024-25 accident year, implying that well under 6% of those who currently qualify for lump-sum compensation would clear the new 25% threshold.
items.so_what: A concentrated spike of pre-reform notifications lands immediately on insurers and the Personal Injury Commission, inflating near-term claim volumes and reserves and front-loading disputes, while the stark drop-off in those meeting the new threshold sets up contested transition cases over lodgement timing.

items.headline: White paper warns tighter workers-comp tests are pushing mental-injury claims into superannuation
items.summary: A University of Melbourne modelling exercise commissioned by EML estimates that tightening workers-compensation eligibility across schemes could drive a roughly 19% rise in superannuation income-protection lodgements over the coming decade if the current legislative trajectory continues nationally, as denied or disputed claims migrate to TPD and income-protection products. Several large super funds have flagged TPD premium increases of up to 40% from June 2026, citing rising disability and mental-health claims.
items.so_what: This reframes the NSW reforms as cost-shifting rather than cost-elimination: liabilities narrowed in the workers-comp scheme may re-emerge in superannuation, raising cross-scheme and reputational risk and inviting scrutiny of whether the reform's savings are real or simply relocated.

items.headline: NSW psychosocial enforcement hardens as codes become an enforceable duty and unions gain civil-penalty standing
items.summary: From 1 July, following an applicable NSW code of practice (or doing something equally effective) becomes a duty in its own right, converting previously advisory psychosocial codes into mandatory compliance benchmarks. Separately, since 1 March 2026 registered organisations including unions can initiate civil-penalty proceedings for WHS breaches, with courts able to direct a share of any penalty to the prosecuting union.
items.so_what: Employers face a higher, enforceable upstream bar on psychosocial risk just as downstream claim entitlements narrow, and union-initiated penalty actions add a new conduct-and-litigation exposure that feeds prevention-spend pressure and potential read-across to insurer risk-selection.
